en.extract:
The white-browed babbler (Pomatostomus superciliosus) is a small, gregarious species of bird in the family Pomatostomidae. They are endemic to the open woodlands and shrubby areas of central and southern Australia. The Latin name superciliosus refers to the supercilium, or 'eyebrow', which is a feature synonymous with the pomatostomine babblers (Hall's babbler, chestnut-crowned babbler, grey-crowned babbler, and white-browed babbler).


Description

Ranging from 17  cm to 21  cm in length, the white-browed babbler is the smallest of the Australian babblers. It is a medium-sized terrestrial bird with a long and decurved bill. The wings are short and rounded in shape, adjacent to a plump, full body which is similar but slightly smaller than the chestnut-crowned or Hall's babbler. The tail is long and graduated, ending with a rounded tip. The gregarious nature of this species means that the tail is often fanned, raised, or a mix of the two. There are many variations to colour; however, the adult babbler generally varies from a dark grey-brown to solid dark brown with distinguished white supercilium and dark brown eye stripe. The underside is usually lighter in colour, varying from light grey or white to light brown; sometimes lighter for females, but generally sexes are similar. Juveniles often have more pronounced dark plumage with a chestnut or cinnamon motif, especially on the underside. Breeding adult males may sometimes have a more pronounced brown 'cap' compared to females or juveniles.


Similar species
Hall's babbler is the species most often confused with the white-browed babbler because of their similarity in size. The two can best be distinguished by comparing the supercilium. Hall's babbler has a narrow and very dark brown crown-stripe with a much broader, vivid white supercilium. Secondly, Hall's babbler does not have a 'fading' eye-stripe towards the rear of the head, where there is more chestnut brown coloration. Individuals will have vivid coloration on the face and will never have a white dot under the eye, which is sometimes present in the white-browed babbler. The tail will also have a square edge rather than a round edge, which the white-browed babbler would have; however, this feature is sometimes not as reliable in juveniles.
Chestnut-crowned babblers are most easily defined by the colour of their cap and wing patterns. They have a much lighter 'chestnut'-colored c cap and distinct double white wing-bars across coverts.
The grey-crowned babbler is significantly larger than its white-browed counterpart. Their size means that to conserve energy, their wing beat pattern changes to more sporadic flapping and gliding rather than even and fast wing beats.


Habitat
The habitat for the white-browed babbler varies across the southern regions of Australia, from dry sclerophyll woodlands, shrublands, heathland, semiarid grasslands and open forests. They prefer areas with a dense understory of shrubs or spinifex for protection and nesting. Their range extends through arid and semiarid areas all the way across the South Australian coastline to Western Australia, and by doing so, it crosses many different ecological vegetation classes (EVCs). No preference seems to be shown with the overall composition of habitat, only that when the understory provides good quality protection from the environment and predators, the species is more successful. Populations have been found in many locations across southern Australia, including dry, rocky gibber desert, mulga, eucalyptusor acacia woodlands Studies have also found many populations in and around human-related infrastructure (see below).
Babblers spend the majority of their time foraging through leaf litter on the ground, although during nesting periods they retreat to nests inside hollowed logs, grass clumps, fallen branches or shrubs.


Distribution
Populations of white-browed babbler live throughout the southern mainland of Australia. Generally, they are located south of the tropic of Capricorn from the east-to-west coast of Australia. The populations that exist in the central areas of Australia tend to be more isolated than those nearer to the coastline.


Behaviour


Breeding
Breeding is widespread throughout the year and across distribution; it depends mostly on environmental conditions. During drought, breeding is less prevalent, or the number of eggs laid can be reduced from four or five to two. Co-operative breeding is common within this species. This is when groups of between two and four breeding pairs and various other individuals form a breeding community, which take turns incubating eggs and repairing nests. Incubation takes around 19 days, followed by a further 20 days developing in the nest.
Breeding pairs are usually monogamous, even though co-operative breeding takes place. Pairs spend almost the entire year in close proximity, preening each other regularly and working in unison to defend territory or feed together. Prospective males perch next to females facing the same direction and begin a loud chatter, jumping from one side of female to the other and then fluttering their wings with their head thrown back. Females then mimic the chatter and wing fluttering. Usually this occurs from late August to mid-September, although can occur at other times before breeding.
Nests are dome-like in shape with a hooded entrance, usually made from small sticks from surrounding environment. Some nests in colder areas have been found to be lined with soft, scavenged furs. On some occasions, they build two nests, one for roosting and the other for brooding; in other words, one for resting and the other for raising young.


Feeding and diet
The diet of the white-browed babbler varies depending on the area that they live in and the availability of food sources. Insects, spiders, amphibians, crustaceans, reptiles, fruit, seeds and nuts have been found to be part of their diet, most commonly varieties of these which can be found on the ground where they spend the majority of their time. Adult feeding individuals have a log or branch from which they hide in and return, rarely venturing from their nest or feeding areas that are known to them. Juveniles without partners tend to be more adventurous while feeding or searching for partners, although they still move between safe areas.


Voice
The most common call heard is a low repeated 'chuck' call, which is thought to be a contact call while foraging. The long-distance call consists of chattering 'mee' sounds repeated three or more times, followed by whistled 'wee-aw' which rises in pitch. As the name suggests, this may be used for communication over long distances with individuals removed from the group; it tends to be much louder than other calls.
When a group is in distress, they all form a chorus of 'churring' sounds. This,  combined with moving from perch to perch, seems to communicate danger and serves as an alarm. There have been studies that suggest that varying numbers of these calls or the speed of them suggest varying levels of danger.


Threats and human interactions
Threats to the species are generally associated with habitat clearing and fragmentation from expanding agriculture and forestry. The most prevalent areas of agriculture are in the Western Australian wheat belt area, where land clearing has led to highly disconnected vegetation. Forestry and extensive bushfires in Victoria are responsible for widespread clearing of remnant vegetation.
Pressure from predation also occurs from introduced species such as cats and foxes. These species, among others, have a complex and widespread effect on the ecosystem. Models that predict the effects of these species suggest that they could affect the numbers of native birds in the long term.
Some studies have found nesting sites on roadsides, golf courses, orchards, agricultural areas and buildings. Other studies indicate that anywhere that understory and groundcover were dense or protection was offered, there were likely to be higher densities of white-browed babbler.


Status and conservation
Commonwealth status: Not listed
State of Victoria: least concern
State of South Australia: least concern
State of Western Australia: least concern
Given the large range of the species, the IUCN has deemed that the population of white-browed babblers in Australia does not approach the thresholds for vulnerability under the range size criterion. The size and density have generally been in decline since the 1970s, although the rate at which this is occurring suggests that there is little need for concern for the species.
Given that the persistence of the species is of little concern, there are no conservation efforts tailored specifically for it; however, there is significant decline of other species in the ecosystems which the white-browed babbler inhabits. Many of these studies suggest that remnant vegetation is integral to the health of these ecosystems and that their removal can cause higher levels of competition in an area. This leads to interspecies competition for remnant vegetation and the species that rely on it decline in number.  Others suggest that the generalist style of feeding is best suited to these degraded landscapes, and therefore the white-browed babbler is least susceptible to degraded conditions.


Also known as
White-eyebrowed babbler, white-browed chatterer, white-browed chatterer, cackler, catbird, chatterer, go-always, happy family, hopping dick, hopping jenny, jumper, kangaroo bird, stickbird and yahoo.


References

The '''white-browed babbler''' ('''''Pomatostomus superciliosus''''') is a small, gregarious species of bird in the family [[Pomatostomidae]].<ref name=Higgins2003>{{cite book|last1=Higgins|first1=P.|last2=Peter|first2=J.|year=2003|title=Handbook of Australian, New Zealand and Antarctic Birds|volume=6|location=Melbourne, Australia|publisher=Oxford University Press}}</ref> They are endemic to the open woodlands and shrubby areas of central and southern Australia.<ref name=Ford1977>{{cite journal|last=Ford|first=J.|year=1977|title=Sympatry in Hall's and White-browed Babblers in New South wales|journal=Emu|volume=77|number=1|page=40|doi=10.1071/MU9770040|bibcode=1977EmuAO..77...40F }}</ref> The Latin name ''superciliosus'' refers to the [[supercilium]], or 'eyebrow', which is a feature synonymous with the ''pomatostomine'' babblers (Hall's babbler, chestnut-crowned babbler, grey-crowned babbler, and white-browed babbler).<ref name=Higgins2003/>

==Description==
[[File:Pomatostomus superciliosus - Glen Alice.jpg|thumb|White-browed babbler]]
Ranging from 17  cm to 21  cm in length, the white-browed babbler is the smallest of the Australian babblers.<ref name=Briggs2007>{{cite journal|last1=Briggs|first1=S. V.|last2=Seddon|first2=J. A.|last3=Doyle|first3=S. J.|year=2007|title=Structures of bird communities in woodland remnants in central New South Wales Australia|journal=Australian Journal of Zoology|volume=55|number=1|pages=29–40|doi=10.1071/ZO06064}}</ref> It is a medium-sized terrestrial bird with a long and decurved bill.<ref name=Briggs2007/> The wings are short and rounded in shape, adjacent to a plump, full body which is similar but slightly smaller than the chestnut-crowned or Hall's babbler.<ref name=Higgins2003/> The tail is long and graduated, ending with a rounded tip.<ref name=Higgins2003/> The gregarious nature of this species means that the tail is often fanned, raised, or a mix of the two.<ref name=Oppenheimer2007>{{cite journal|last1=Oppenheimer|first1=S.|last2=Astheimer|first2=L.|last3=Anderson|first3=M. J.|last4=Buttemer|first4=B.|year=2007|title=Morphometric measures as a moderate predictor of gender in the white-browed babbler Pomatostomus superciliosus gilgandra|journal=Corella|volume=31|number=1|pages=15–18}}</ref> There are many variations to colour; however, the adult babbler generally varies from a dark grey-brown to solid dark brown with distinguished white supercilium and dark brown eye stripe.<ref name=Higgins2003/> The underside is usually lighter in colour, varying from light grey or white to light brown; sometimes lighter for females, but generally sexes are similar.<ref name=Higgins2003/> Juveniles often have more pronounced dark [[plumage]] with a chestnut or cinnamon motif, especially on the underside. Breeding adult males may sometimes have a more pronounced brown 'cap' compared to females or juveniles.<ref name=Thomson2007>{{cite journal|last1=Thomson|first1=J. R.|last2=Mac Nally|first2=R.|last3=Fleishman|first3=E.|last4=Horrocks|first4=G.|year=2007|title=Predicting Bird Species Distributions in Reconstructed Landscapes|journal=Conservation Biology|volume=21|number=3|pages=752–766|doi=10.1111/j.1523-1739.2007.00687.x|pmid=17531053|bibcode=2007ConBi..21..752T }}</ref>

===Similar species===
[[Hall's babbler]] is the species most often confused with the white-browed babbler because of their similarity in size. The two can best be distinguished by comparing the supercilium. Hall's babbler has a narrow and very dark brown crown-stripe with a much broader, vivid white supercilium.<ref name=Miura2001>{{cite journal|last1=Miura|first1=G.I.|last2=Edwards|first2=S.V.|year=2001|title=Cryptic differentiation and geographic variation in genetic diversity of Hall's Babbler Pomatostomus halli|journal=Journal of Avian Biology|volume=32|number=2|pages=102–110|doi=10.1034/j.1600-048X.2001.320202.x}}</ref> Secondly, Hall's babbler does not have a 'fading' eye-stripe towards the rear of the head, where there is more chestnut brown coloration.<ref name=Gill1983>{{cite journal|last1=Gill|first1=B.|last2=Dow|first2=D.|year=1983|title=Morphology and Development of Nestling Grey-crowned and Hall's Babblers|journal=Emu|volume=83|number=1|pages=41–43|doi=10.1071/MU9830041|bibcode=1983EmuAO..83...41G }}</ref> Individuals will have vivid coloration on the face and will never have a white dot under the eye, which is sometimes present in the white-browed babbler.<ref name=Brown1977>{{cite journal|last1=Brown|first1=J.L.|last2=Balda|first2=R.P.|year=1977|title=The Relationship of Habitat Quality to Group Size in Hall's Babbler (Pomatostomus halli)|journal=The Condor|volume=79|number=3|pages=312–320|doi=10.2307/1368008|jstor=1368008}}</ref> The tail will also have a square edge rather than a round edge, which the white-browed babbler would have; however, this feature is sometimes not as reliable in juveniles.<ref name=Gill1983/>

[[Chestnut-crowned babbler]]s are most easily defined by the colour of their cap and wing patterns.<ref name=Higgins2003/> They have a much lighter 'chestnut'-colored c cap and distinct double white wing-bars across coverts.<ref name=Higgins2003/>

The [[grey-crowned babbler]] is significantly larger than its white-browed counterpart.<ref name=Gill1983/> Their size means that to conserve energy, their wing beat pattern changes to more sporadic flapping and gliding rather than even and fast wing beats.<ref name=Gill1983/>

==Habitat==
The habitat for the white-browed babbler varies across the southern regions of Australia, from [[dry sclerophyll]] woodlands, shrublands, heathland, semiarid grasslands and open forests.<ref name=Briggs2007/><ref name=Allen2002>{{cite journal|last1=Allen|first1=C.R.|last2=Saunders|first2=D.A.|year=2002|title=Variability between Scales: Predictors of Nomadism in Birds of an Australian Mediterranean-climate Ecosystem|journal=Ecosystems|volume=5|number=4|pages=348–359|doi=10.1007/s10021-001-0079-z|bibcode=2002Ecosy...5..348A |s2cid=14082325|url=http://digitalcommons.unl.edu/ncfwrustaff/23|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref name=Pavey2009>{{cite journal|last1=Pavey|first1=C.R.|last2=Nano|first2=C.E.M.|year=2009|title=Bird assemblages of arid Australia: Vegetation patterns have a greater effect than disturbance and resource pulses|journal=Journal of Arid Environments|volume=73|number=6–7|pages=634–642|doi=10.1016/j.jaridenv.2009.01.010|bibcode=2009JArEn..73..634P}}</ref> They prefer areas with a dense [[understory]] of shrubs or spinifex for protection and nesting.<ref name=Taylor2006>{{Cite journal|last1=Taylor|first1=S.G.|last2=Paul|first2=W.L.|year=2006|title=Minimal diurnal change in foraging time in an Australian passerine, the white-browed babbler Pomatostomus superciliosus|journal=Journal of Avian Biology|volume=37|number=5|pages=527–531|doi=10.1111/j.0908-8857.2006.03530.x}}</ref> Their range extends through arid and semiarid areas all the way across the South Australian coastline to Western Australia, and by doing so, it crosses many different ecological vegetation classes (EVCs).<ref name=Higgins2003/> No preference seems to be shown with the overall composition of habitat, only that when the understory provides good quality protection from the environment and predators, the species is more successful.<ref name=Ford1977/><ref name=Brown1977/> Populations have been found in many locations across southern Australia, including dry, rocky gibber desert, mulga, eucalyptusor acacia woodlands<ref name=Higgins2003/> Studies have also found many populations in and around human-related infrastructure (see below).<ref name=Arnold1998>{{cite journal|last1=Arnold|first1=G.W.|last2=Weeldenburg|first2=J.R.|year=1998|title=The effects of isolation, habitat fragmentation and degradation by livestock grazing on the use by birds of patches of Gimlet Eucalyptus salubris woodland in the wheatbelt of Western Australia|journal=Pacific Conservation Biology|volume=4|number=2|pages=155–163|doi=10.1071/PC980155 |bibcode=1998PacSB...4..155W }}</ref><ref name=Fulton2006>{{cite journal|last1=Fulton|first1=G.R.|last2=Majer|first2=J.D.|year=2006|title=The effect of recent chaining on birds in the eastern wheatbelt of Western Australia|journal=Pacific Conservation Biology|volume=12|number=3|pages=168–174|doi=10.1071/PC060168 |bibcode=2006PacSB..12..168R }}</ref><ref name=Lynch1996>{{cite book|last1=Lynch|first1=J.F.|last2=Carmen|first2=W.J.|last3=Saunders|first3=D.A.|last4=Cale|first4=P.|year=1996|chapter=Use of vegetated road verges and habitat patches by four bird species in the central wheatbelt of Western Australia|title=Nature Conservation 4: The role of networks|publisher=Surrey Beatty|isbn=978-0-949324-65-8|pages=34–42}}</ref>

Babblers spend the majority of their time foraging through leaf litter on the ground, although during nesting periods they retreat to nests inside hollowed logs, grass clumps, fallen branches or shrubs.<ref name=Antos2006>{{cite journal|last1=Antos|first1=M.J.|last2=Bennett|first2=A.F.|year=2006|title=Foraging ecology of ground-feeding woodland birds in temperate woodlands of southern Australia|journal=Emu|volume=106|number=1|pages=29–40|doi=10.1071/MU05039|bibcode=2006EmuAO.106...29A |s2cid=84827833}}</ref>

==Distribution==
Populations of white-browed babbler live throughout the southern mainland of Australia.<ref name=Higgins2003/> Generally, they are located south of the tropic of Capricorn from the east-to-west coast of Australia. The populations that exist in the central areas of Australia tend to be more isolated than those nearer to the coastline.<ref name=Higgins2003/>

==Behaviour==

===Breeding===
Breeding is widespread throughout the year and across distribution; it depends mostly on environmental conditions.<ref name=Higgins2003/> During drought, breeding is less prevalent, or the number of eggs laid can be reduced from four or five to two.<ref name=Cunningham2008>{{cite journal|last1=Cunningham|first1=R.B.|last2=Lindenmayer|first2=D.B.|last3=Crane|first3=M.|last4=Michael|first4=D.|last5=MacGregor|first5=C.|last6=Montague-Drake|first6=R.|last7=Fischer|first7=J.|year=2008|title=The Combined Effects of Remnant Vegetation and Tree Planting on Farmland Birds|journal=Conservation Biology|volume=22|number=3|pages=742–752|doi=10.1111/j.1523-1739.2008.00924.x|pmid=18477028|doi-access=free|bibcode=2008ConBi..22..742C }}</ref> Co-operative breeding is common within this species. This is when groups of between two and four breeding pairs and various other individuals form a breeding community, which take turns incubating eggs and repairing nests.<ref name=MacNally2002>{{cite journal|last1=MacNally|first1=R.|last2=Horrocks|first2=G.|last3=Bennett|first3=A.F.|year=2002|title=Nestedness in fragmented landscapes: birds of the box-ironbark forests of south-eastern Australia|journal=Ecography|volume=25|number=6|pages=651–660|doi=10.1034/j.1600-0587.2002.250602.x|bibcode=2002Ecogr..25..651M }}</ref> Incubation takes around 19 days, followed by a further 20 days developing in the nest.<ref name=Higgins2003/>

Breeding pairs are usually monogamous, even though co-operative breeding takes place.<ref name=Higgins2003/> Pairs spend almost the entire year in close proximity, preening each other regularly and working in unison to defend territory or feed together.<ref name=Higgins2003/><ref name=Antos2008>{{cite journal|last1=Antos|first1=M.J.|last2=Bennett|first2=A.F.|last3=White|first3=J.G.|year=2008|title=Where exactly do ground-foraging woodland birds forage? Foraging sites and microhabitat selection in temperate woodlands of southern Australia|journal=Emu|volume=108|number=3|pages=201–211|doi=10.1071/MU08005|bibcode=2008EmuAO.108..201A |s2cid=85170127}}</ref> Prospective males perch next to females facing the same direction and begin a loud chatter, jumping from one side of female to the other and then fluttering their wings with their head thrown back. Females then mimic the chatter and wing fluttering.<ref name=Taylor2006/> Usually this occurs from late August to mid-September, although can occur at other times before breeding.<ref name=Taylor2006/>

Nests are dome-like in shape with a hooded entrance, usually made from small sticks from surrounding environment.<ref name=Higgins2003/> Some nests in colder areas have been found to be lined with soft, scavenged furs.<ref name=Gill1983/> On some occasions, they build two nests, one for roosting and the other for brooding; in other words, one for resting and the other for raising young.<ref name=MacNally2002/>

===Feeding and diet===
The diet of the white-browed babbler varies depending on the area that they live in and the availability of food sources.<ref name=Luck1999>{{cite journal|last1=Luck|first1=G.W.|last2=Possingham|first2=H.P.|last3=Paton|first3=D.C.|year=1999|title=Bird responses at inherent and induced edges in the Murray Mallee, South Australia. 1. Differences in abundance and diversity|journal=Emu|volume=99|number=3|pages=157–169|doi=10.1071/mu99019|bibcode=1999EmuAO..99..157L }}</ref> Insects, spiders, amphibians, crustaceans, reptiles, fruit, seeds and nuts have been found to be part of their diet, most commonly varieties of these which can be found on the ground where they spend the majority of their time.<ref name=Antos2006/> Adult feeding individuals have a log or branch from which they hide in and return, rarely venturing from their nest or feeding areas that are known to them.<ref name=Antos2006/> Juveniles without partners tend to be more adventurous while feeding or searching for partners, although they still move between safe areas.<ref name=Antos2006/>

===Voice===
The most common call heard is a low repeated 'chuck' call, which is thought to be a contact call while foraging.<ref name=Antos2008/> The long-distance call consists of chattering 'mee' sounds repeated three or more times, followed by whistled 'wee-aw' which rises in pitch. As the name suggests, this may be used for communication over long distances with individuals removed from the group; it tends to be much louder than other calls.<ref name=Higgins2003/>

When a group is in distress, they all form a chorus of 'churring' sounds. This,  combined with moving from perch to perch, seems to communicate danger and serves as an alarm.<ref name=Higgins2003/> There have been studies that suggest that varying numbers of these calls or the speed of them suggest varying levels of danger.<ref name=Higgins2003/>

==Threats and human interactions==
Threats to the species are generally associated with habitat clearing and fragmentation from expanding agriculture and forestry.<ref name=Pavey2009/> The most prevalent areas of agriculture are in the Western Australian wheat belt area, where land clearing has led to highly disconnected vegetation.<ref name=Arnold1998/><ref name=Lynch1996/> Forestry and extensive bushfires in Victoria are responsible for widespread clearing of remnant vegetation.<ref name=Attiwill1994>{{cite journal|last=Attiwill|first=P.M.|year=1994|title=Ecological disturbance and the conservative management of eucalypt forests in Australia|journal=Forest Ecology and Management|volume=63|number=2–3|pages=301–346|doi=10.1016/0378-1127(94)90115-5|bibcode=1994ForEM..63..301A }}</ref>

Pressure from predation also occurs from [[introduced species]] such as cats and foxes.<ref name=Short2002>{{cite journal|last1=Short|first1=J.|last2=Kinnear|first2=J.E.|last3=Robley|first3=A|year=2002|title=Surplus killing by introduced predators in Australia—evidence for ineffective anti-predator adaptations in native prey species?|journal=Biological Conservation|volume=103|number=3|pages=283–301|doi=10.1016/S0006-3207(01)00139-2|bibcode=2002BCons.103..283S }}</ref> These species, among others, have a complex and widespread effect on the ecosystem.<ref name=Pavey2009/> Models that predict the effects of these species suggest that they could affect the numbers of native birds in the long term.<ref name=Pavey2009/>

Some studies have found nesting sites on roadsides, golf courses, orchards, agricultural areas and buildings.<ref name=Pavey2009/><ref name=Antos2008/><ref name=James2003>{{cite journal|last=James|first=C.D.|year=2003|title=Response of vertebrates to fenceline contrasts in grazing intensity in semi-arid woodlands of eastern Australia|journal=Austral Ecology|volume=28|number=2|pages=137–151|doi=10.1046/j.1442-9993.2003.01259.x|bibcode=2003AusEc..28..137J }}</ref> Other studies indicate that anywhere that understory and [[groundcover]] were dense or protection was offered, there were likely to be higher densities of white-browed babbler.<ref name=Pavey2009/>

==Status and conservation==
*Commonwealth status: Not listed
*State of Victoria: least concern
*State of South Australia: least concern
*State of Western Australia: least concern<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

Given the large range of the species, the IUCN has deemed that the population of white-browed babblers in Australia does not approach the thresholds for vulnerability under the range size criterion.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> The size and density have generally been in decline since the 1970s, although the rate at which this is occurring suggests that there is little need for concern for the species.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

Given that the persistence of the species is of little concern, there are no conservation efforts tailored specifically for it; however, there is significant decline of other species in the ecosystems which the white-browed babbler inhabits.<ref name=Taylor2006/> Many of these studies suggest that remnant vegetation is integral to the health of these ecosystems and that their removal can cause higher levels of competition in an area.<ref name=Briggs2007/><ref name=Cunningham2008/><ref name=Cooney2006>{{cite journal|last1=Cooney|first1=S.J.N.|last2=Watson|first2=D.M.|last3=Young|first3=J.|year=2006|title=Mistletoe nesting in Australian birds: a review|journal=Emu|volume=106|number=1|pages=1–12|doi=10.1071/MU04018|bibcode=2006EmuAO.106....1C |s2cid=84296716}}</ref> This leads to interspecies competition for remnant vegetation and the species that rely on it decline in number.  Others suggest that the generalist style of feeding is best suited to these degraded landscapes, and therefore the white-browed babbler is least susceptible to degraded conditions.<ref name=Briggs2007/><ref name=Antos2006/>

==Also known as==
White-eyebrowed babbler, white-browed chatterer, white-browed chatterer, cackler, catbird, chatterer, go-always, happy family, hopping dick, hopping jenny, jumper, kangaroo bird, stickbird and yahoo.<ref name=Higgins2003/>

zh.extract:
白眉弯嘴鹛（学名：Pomatostomus superciliosus），是雀形目弯嘴鹛科的一种鸟类。
白眉弯嘴鹛体重约41.53克，翼长约78.1毫米，嘴峰长约28毫米，喙宽度约4毫米，喙厚度约5.8毫米，跗蹠长约26.7毫米，尾长约79.7毫米。白眉弯嘴鹛在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在半开放的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为肉食性，主要食物来源是陆生无脊椎动物。


亚种
P. s. gilgandra：分布于澳大利亚东部的昆士兰州南部至维多利亚州北部。
P. s. superciliosus：分布于澳大利亚南部新南威尔士州西部和维多利亚州西部等地区。
P. s. ashbyi：分布于西澳大利亚州西南部。
P. s. centralis：分布于西澳大利亚州中部至北领地西南部和南澳大利亚州西北部。
